title: Willow fishing creel
subtitle: Traditional round-belly willow creel for freshwater fishing
excerpt: A willow fishing creel in the English round-belly tradition: a kidney-shaped form worn on the back with a lid that folds back and a slit opening in the lid for dropping in fish. The construction is advanced; the kidney base is the most complex basket-base form; but the result is a working tool with a history stretching back centuries.
sourceNotes: Victorian fishing and field-sports manuals for creel forms; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for wickerwork construction; the round-belly creel is documented in museum collections at the Museum of English Rural Life.

glossaryTerms.definition: Willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes.
A tight basketry weave using three or more rods together, used to strengthen transitions and the rim.
The crossing of base sticks at the start of a willow basket base. The kidney base uses a modified slath to produce the curved form.
The first few rows of waling worked immediately after staking-up; the upsett locks the stakes and sets the basket footprint.
Inserting the upright stakes alongside the base spokes to form the basket sides.

content.text: A fishing creel is a basket designed to be worn; the kidney shape fits against the back and the rounded belly faces outward, keeping the fish cool and aerated on a warm day. The construction is the most demanding in the basketry repertoire: the kidney base requires modifying the standard
to produce a curved footprint, and the
on a convex base requires holding the stakes at two different angles simultaneously. A confident intermediate basketmaker should expect to spend a full day on a first creel.

Brown or buff willow, 3-foot and 4-foot lengths for the sides and lid; heavy grade 4-foot and 5-foot lengths for the base. Approximately 1.5 kg of willow for a standard 40 cm creel. Leather or wide webbing for the back straps.

Build the base using 8 base sticks arranged in a kidney footprint: 5 sticks at 40 cm for the long axis, 3 sticks at 30 cm for the short axis, joined at a slight angle rather than in a straight cross. The angle creates the curve. Tie the
and work 5 rows of pairing weave around the base sticks, spacing the spokes at 4 cm intervals. The finished base should measure 40 cm along the back and 28 cm across.
Insert two stakes beside each base spoke. The back stakes (the flat side of the kidney) are held vertical; the front stakes (the curved belly) are held at 20 degrees outward. The
waling locks both angles simultaneously: 4 rows of three-rod waling, worked with the stakes held at the correct angles throughout.
to 30 cm depth at the back and 35 cm at the front belly. The extra height at the front creates the rounded belly shape when viewed from the side. Finish the rim with 4 rows of three-rod

Work a flat lid on the same kidney footprint as the base; 5 to 6 cm deep with a slitting opening 8 cm long and 3 cm wide near the front, through which fish are inserted. Hinge the lid at the back with leather loops stitched through the rim willow and the lid border.

Attach two back straps in leather or webbing at the top and bottom of the back face of the creel. The straps should be 3 cm wide and adjustable. The creel sits against the lower back; the straps cross the shoulders like a rucksack.
Smaller creel (30 cm): proportionally reduced; suitable for stream trout fishing rather than river fishing.
Display creel: without the back straps; hung on a wall or used as a decorative bread or magazine basket.

Rinse the creel with fresh water after use and leave to dry in open air. Oiling the leather straps annually extends their life. A willow creel used regularly in water and sunlight will last 10 to 15 years with ordinary maintenance.

items.symptom: Kidney shape becomes oval during randing
items.cause: Even tension applied all around the creel during randing; the back requires slightly less tension than the front
items.fix: Rap the back rows slightly less firmly than the front rows. The difference in tension helps maintain the kidney footprint as the sides rise.
